visualizations.title: The Great Convergence
visualizations.description: Life expectancy at birth in 1960 vs 2023 for 21 countries. Sixty years ago, a child born in Japan could expect 34 more years of life than one born in Afghanistan. Today that gap has narrowed to 18 years.
highlights: China gained 44.5 years of life expectancy, the largest absolute gain of any country shown
Iran, Afghanistan, and Bangladesh each gained more than 30 years
Rich nations in 1960 gained 11-16 years; poor nations gained 15-44 years, showing convergence
Chad and Central African Republic remain below 58 years despite gains

visualizations.title: Sixty Years of Saved Children
visualizations.description: The decline in under-5 mortality rate (per 1,000 live births) for 18 countries from the 1960s to 2023. Each line traces one country's trajectory, ordered from highest historical mortality to lowest.
highlights: Afghanistan's under-5 mortality fell from 353 to 56 per 1,000 live births
Bangladesh dropped from 262 to 31 -- a 10x improvement
Kenya shows a striking reversal in the 1990s (HIV/AIDS crisis) before resuming decline
Japan reached 2 per 1,000 -- nearly eliminating child mortality entirely

visualizations.title: The Preston Curve, Unwound
visualizations.description: Six countries traced through GDP per capita and life expectancy from 1960 to 2023. Each line shows how wealth and health evolved together over six decades.
highlights: China's trail shows the most dramatic transformation: from $90 GDP/cap and 33 years life expectancy to $13,000 and 78 years
Japan's trail bends backward after 1995 as GDP stagnated while life expectancy kept rising
Nigeria's path barely moves upward despite GDP fluctuations, stuck around 50-55 years
Brazil and India trace parallel upward paths but at different wealth levels
The US trail flattens at the top: high wealth but diminishing life expectancy gains

visualizations.title: The Digital Divide, Closing
visualizations.description: Internet adoption as a percentage of the population, circa 2000 vs the latest available year, for 33 countries. Each row shows one country's transformation from the early web to near-universal connectivity.
highlights: Kazakhstan went from 0.7% to 93.4% internet adoption -- a 133x increase
China went from 1.8% to 92% -- connecting over a billion people
Albania jumped from 0.1% to 83.1%, one of the largest relative gains
Kenya and Bangladesh remain below 50%, showing the divide persists in parts of South Asia and Africa

visualizations.title: Development at a Glance
visualizations.description: Six development indicators across 19 countries. Dot size shows magnitude, color shows relative standing. 114 data points in a single compact view.
highlights: Ethiopia has 95.4% renewable electricity but the lowest life expectancy and internet access in the group
Japan leads in life expectancy (84 years) and lowest child mortality (2.4 per 1,000) but has only 21% renewable electricity
Bangladesh and India show similar GDP per capita (~$2,600) but Bangladesh has higher child mortality
Brazil stands out with 77% renewable electricity, high urbanization, and strong internet adoption

visualizations.title: The Demographic Transition
visualizations.description: Fertility rate (births per woman) from 1960 to 2023 for 10 countries. Every country shown has fallen toward or below the replacement level of 2.1.
highlights: South Korea fell from 4.5 births per woman in 1970 to 0.72 in 2023 -- the lowest in the world
Iran's drop was the fastest: from 7.5 to 1.7, mostly in a single decade (1985-1995)
China's one-child policy shows as a sharp cliff from 6.6 in 1965 to 3.6 in 1975
Ethiopia remains the only country shown still above 3, but is falling rapidly

visualizations.title: The Price of a Year of Life
visualizations.description: Health spending per capita vs life expectancy across 20 countries. Some achieve long lives cheaply. Others spend enormously for modest returns.
highlights: Cuba achieves 78.1 years of life expectancy spending just $1,199 per person -- rivaling countries that spend 5x more
Germany and Australia spend $6,800-7,000 per person but trail Japan and Italy in life expectancy
Bangladesh reaches 74.7 years on just $53 per person -- the highest efficiency in the dataset
Ethiopia spends $35 per person, the least of any country shown, and has the shortest life expectancy at 67.3 years

visualizations.title: Women at Work
visualizations.description: Female labor force participation (% of women 15+), 1990 vs latest, for 18 countries. Some leapt forward, others barely moved, a few went backward.
highlights: Chile saw the largest gain: from 31% to 52.8%, a 21.8 percentage point increase
China and Ethiopia both declined -- from over 73% to under 60% -- as economies restructured
Iran started at 9.6% in 1990 and rose only to 14.1% by 2024
South Korea, Japan, and Germany all converged near 55-57%, from very different starting points

visualizations.title: Who Burns, Who Breathes
visualizations.description: CO2 emissions from fuel combustion (millions of tonnes), 2000-2022, for the top 10 emitting countries. Traces the dramatic rise of Asian economies and the slow decline of Western industrial emitters.
highlights: China's emissions tripled from ~3,200 Mt in 2001 to over 10,500 Mt in 2021, overtaking the United States around 2006
The United States has fallen steadily from a peak of ~5,700 Mt in 2005 to ~4,550 Mt in 2021, a 20% reduction
India is the only top-10 emitter still on a steep upward trajectory, more than doubling from 1,000 Mt to 2,500 Mt
Germany cut emissions by nearly 25% over the period, the sharpest decline among large economies shown

visualizations.title: Europe's Green Surge
visualizations.description: Share of renewable energy in gross final energy consumption for 20 European countries, comparing 2004 vs 2024. Each row shows one country's two-decade journey toward cleaner energy.
highlights: Iceland and Norway already led in 2004 with ~58% renewable share and have since exceeded 77-79%
Denmark made the largest gain among major EU members, nearly tripling from 14.8% to 46.5%
Greece, Germany, and Spain started below 10% in 2004 and have all crossed 22-25% by 2024
Sweden and Finland now derive over 50% and 62% of gross final energy from renewables respectively

visualizations.title: The Inequality Spectrum
visualizations.description: Gini index (income inequality) for 25 countries using the most recent available data (2020-2024). Countries ranked from most to least unequal, color-coded from red (high inequality) to green (low).
highlights: Colombia (53.9) and Brazil (51.6) are the most unequal countries in the dataset, both above 50
Latin America dominates the top 12 positions, with six countries spanning the 42-46 range
Northern European countries cluster at the low end: Denmark (29.9), France (31.8), Germany (32.4)
The global range spans nearly 30 points, underscoring vast differences in income distribution

visualizations.title: The Malaria Frontier
visualizations.description: Estimated malaria incidence per 1,000 population at risk across 16 high-burden countries from 2000 to 2024. Most of Sub-Saharan Africa has seen real but uneven progress.
highlights: Senegal achieved the steepest decline, falling from 177 to 35 per 1,000 -- an 80% reduction
Ethiopia cut its incidence by 76%, from 189 to 46 per 1,000
Tanzania and Ghana each dropped by more than 55%
Niger and Mali made the least progress, remaining above 300-340 per 1,000 through 2023

visualizations.title: The Safety Divide
visualizations.description: Intentional homicide rate per 100,000 population for 25 countries spanning the full global spectrum (2020-2023). A 1,000-fold difference separates the most and least violent nations.
highlights: Saint Lucia has the highest rate at 68.85 per 100,000 -- nearly 1,000 times higher than Singapore's 0.07
Latin America and the Caribbean dominate the top 12 positions
The United States (8.94) sits far above Western European peers like Germany (0.87) and France (1.94)
East Asian and Gulf states cluster near zero: Japan 0.23, Singapore 0.07

visualizations.title: Water Under Stress
visualizations.description: Freshwater withdrawal as a percentage of available renewable freshwater resources for 28 countries (2022). Countries exceeding 100% depend on desalination or fossil groundwater.
highlights: Kuwait withdraws 3,850% of its renewable freshwater, entirely dependent on desalination
Egypt (141%) and Algeria (98%) are the only African nations in critical or near-critical territory
India at 60% and Israel at 56% approach high-stress thresholds despite very different economic contexts
Most European countries fall well below the 25% moderate-stress threshold

visualizations.title: Mind the Gap
visualizations.description: Gender pay gap (unadjusted) across 27 EU member states, comparing the earliest Eurostat measurement (2002-2010) with 2024. In most countries the gap has narrowed, but women still earn 10-19% less on average.
highlights: Spain reduced its gap most dramatically: from 20.2% to 7.3%, a 13 percentage point improvement
Estonia remains the EU's widest gap despite falling from 29.8% to 18.8%
Luxembourg is the only EU country where the gap has effectively closed: women now earn 0.8% more
Three countries -- Slovenia, Croatia, and Italy -- saw their gap widen slightly

visualizations.title: The Vaccine Shield
visualizations.description: DTP3 immunization coverage among 1-year-olds (%) for 12 countries from 2000 to 2024. Conflict, instability, and health system strength produce starkly different trajectories.
highlights: North Korea climbed from 56% in 2000 to 99% by 2021
Brazil and Romania both fell from near-universal coverage (98-99%) to below 80%
India rose steadily from 59% to 91%, crossing the WHO 90% target threshold by 2023
Yemen and Papua New Guinea remain far below the 90% target with no sign of recovery

visualizations.title: Gaining Ground, Losing Ground
visualizations.description: Forest area as a percentage of land, 1990 vs 2023, for 22 countries. Dramatic losses in Southeast Asia and Central Africa alongside deliberate reforestation in China, France, and Costa Rica.
highlights: Cambodia lost 19.3 percentage points of forest cover, the steepest decline in the selection
China gained 7.3 pp through state-led afforestation, rising from 16.7% to 24.0%
Costa Rica reversed decades of deforestation through payment-for-ecosystem-services programs
France quietly expanded its forests by 5.7 pp while tropical nations lost more than 12 pp

visualizations.title: Lighting Up the World
visualizations.description: Access to electricity (% of population) from 1990 to 2023 for 12 countries in Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia that started with limited access and show dramatic improvement.
highlights: Bangladesh rose from 14% electricity access in 1991 to 99.5% by 2023
Bhutan reached universal access (100%) by 2018, driven by hydropower development
Cambodia went from under 20% in the late 1990s to 95% by 2023
Ethiopia has more than quadrupled coverage to 55% but is still climbing

visualizations.title: Leaving Poverty Behind
visualizations.description: Proportion of population living below the international poverty line ($2.15/day) for 13 countries, 1977-2024. Countries that lifted hundreds of millions out of poverty alongside those where progress has stalled.
highlights: China reduced its extreme poverty rate from 97% in 1981 to 0% by 2019
Nepal fell from 82.7% in 1984 to just 2.6% in 2022
Madagascar and Mozambique have seen poverty rates above 87%, essentially unchanged for three decades
Ethiopia's poverty rate dropped sharply to 16% by 2010, then rebounded to 45% by 2021 amid conflict

visualizations.title: The Health Divide
visualizations.description: A dot-matrix scorecard comparing 15 countries across four health indicators: malaria incidence, DTP3 immunization, maternal mortality, and HIV prevalence. Color and size encode relative performance.
highlights: Nigeria carries the heaviest combined burden: highest malaria incidence, lowest DTP3 coverage, worst maternal mortality
India and Bangladesh stand out as South Asian bright spots with near-zero malaria and high vaccination rates
Ghana achieves 95% DTP3 coverage despite a high malaria burden of 195 per 1,000
Kenya has the highest HIV prevalence at 3.0% while Bangladesh and India report below 0.2%

visualizations.title: Forced From Home
visualizations.description: Number of refugees by country of origin from 2000 to 2024, highlighting the world's largest displacement crises. UNHCR data traces the rise and fall of refugee populations from Syria, Afghanistan, Ukraine, and other conflict zones.
highlights: Syria's refugee population exploded from near zero in 2011 to over 6.5 million by 2016, making it the world's largest source of refugees
Afghanistan has been a top-3 refugee source for over two decades, with numbers climbing back above 6 million after the 2021 Taliban takeover
Ukraine generated the fastest-ever refugee crisis, surging from negligible numbers to over 6 million in a single year (2022)
South Sudan and Myanmar each produced over a million refugees following civil conflict and military coups

visualizations.title: Where Joy Lives
visualizations.description: Happiness scores from the Cantril Ladder (0-10 scale) for 30+ countries. Self-reported life satisfaction from the Gallup World Poll, showing which countries report the highest and lowest wellbeing.
highlights: Finland tops the rankings with a score of 7.76, continuing its streak as the world's happiest country
Nordic countries dominate the top 5, all scoring above 7.2
Afghanistan scores lowest at around 1.7, reflecting decades of conflict and instability
Costa Rica scores 7.44, the highest among non-European nations and higher than many wealthy countries

visualizations.title: The Debt Mountain
visualizations.description: Government gross debt as a share of GDP, 2000-2030, for 10 major economies. Values after 2024 are IMF projections. Traces the long-term fiscal trajectories of the world's largest economies through crises and recoveries.
highlights: Japan's debt peaked at 258% of GDP in 2020 and remains the highest among major economies even after declining to 236%
China's debt trajectory is the steepest, projected to cross 100% of GDP by 2026 from just 23% in 2000
The COVID-19 pandemic caused visible debt spikes across all countries in 2020, adding 15-25 percentage points
Germany stands out as the only major economy that meaningfully reduced its debt ratio from 2010-2019

visualizations.title: Democracy's Ebb and Flow
visualizations.description: Electoral Democracy Index (0-1 scale) for 12 countries from 1970 to 2025, grouped by regime trajectory: stable democracies (Sweden, USA), democratization stories (Chile, South Korea, South Africa), democratic backsliders (Hungary, Poland, Turkey, India), and persistent autocracies (Russia, China, Saudi Arabia).
highlights: Hungary and Poland surged past 0.85 after 1990 but have slid sharply since 2010-2016 under Orban and PiS; Poland recovered noticeably after 2023
Turkey peaked near 0.66 in 2004 during EU-accession reforms, then fell below 0.3 by 2017 following Erdogan's consolidation of power
Chile and South Korea both transitioned from sub-0.15 autocracy to above-0.8 democracy within a single decade (1987-1993)
Saudi Arabia has remained flat near 0.015 for 55 years -- the least democratic score in the dataset throughout the entire period

visualizations.title: The Price of Prices
visualizations.description: Inflation rate (annual % change in consumer prices) for 10 countries from 2000 to 2030. Uses a symlog scale to handle Venezuela's 65,374% hyperinflation alongside Japan's deflation. IMF projections shown as dashed lines.
highlights: Venezuela's inflation peaked at 65,374% in 2018 before collapsing under dollarization
Argentina surged to 220% in 2024 before IMF projects a dramatic decline under Milei's reforms
Turkey tamed inflation to single digits by 2004 only to see it resurge past 72% in 2022
Japan spent over two decades in deflation, only recently seeing positive inflation

visualizations.title: When Nature Strikes
visualizations.description: Global deaths from natural disasters per year, 1900-2024. Aggregated across all countries. The deadliest years are annotated with their major events: China floods, Bengal famine, Indian Ocean tsunami, Haiti earthquake.
highlights: The 1931 China floods killed 3.7 million people, the deadliest single disaster year in the dataset
The 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami (242K dead) and 2010 Haiti earthquake (308K) stand out in the modern era
Deaths have trended dramatically downward despite population growth, thanks to early warning systems and better infrastructure
The 1928 and 1943 famines in China and Bengal each killed over 1.9 million

visualizations.title: The Atomic Divide
visualizations.description: Nuclear share of electricity generation for 10 countries from 2000 to 2025. A vertical annotation marks the 2011 Fukushima disaster, after which Japan's nuclear share collapsed from 25% to near zero.
highlights: France maintains the world's highest nuclear share at ~65%, though down from 77% in 2005
Japan's nuclear share crashed from 25% to near zero after Fukushima and has only partially recovered to ~8%
Germany completed its nuclear phase-out, dropping from 30% to 0% by 2023
China is the only country rapidly expanding nuclear, growing from 1% to over 5%

visualizations.title: Guns and Butter
visualizations.description: Military expenditure as % of GDP, 2002 vs 2024, for 22 countries. Slope chart showing which nations are rearming (coral) and which are spending less on defense (green). NATO rearmament and post-conflict drawdowns both visible.
highlights: Israel increased from 6.37% to 8.78% of GDP on defense following regional conflicts
Algeria nearly tripled its military spending share from 3.41% to 7.97%
Estonia doubled from 1.66% to 3.37%, leading NATO's rearmament push
Ethiopia made the sharpest cut, from 3.76% to just 0.65%

visualizations.title: Bottoms Up
visualizations.description: Alcohol consumption per capita (litres pure alcohol per year, ages 15+) for 25 countries. Horizontal bar chart colored by consumption level, from Czech Republic's 12.85L to Iran's 0.0L.
highlights: Czech Republic leads at 12.85 litres per capita, followed closely by France (12.6) and Ireland (12.51)
Russia at 7.03L is lower than many Western European countries, reflecting anti-alcohol campaigns
Muslim-majority countries (Iran, Pakistan, Indonesia) cluster near zero
India reports just 0.002L per capita, likely reflecting underreporting and large abstinent populations

visualizations.title: Investing in Minds
visualizations.description: Government education spending as % of GDP for 26 countries. Dot plot sorted from highest (Cuba at 8.44%) to lowest (Sri Lanka at 1.83%), with a vertical reference line at the ~4.5% global average.
highlights: Cuba spends 8.44% of GDP on education, nearly double the global average
Botswana and South Africa are the top African spenders at 7.47% and 6.34% respectively
Japan spends just 3.26% of GDP, well below the global average despite its education reputation
Pakistan and Myanmar spend under 2%, among the lowest in the world

visualizations.title: Displaced Within
visualizations.description: Internally displaced persons (IDPs) for the top 10 countries from 2000 to 2024. Stacked area chart showing how the global IDP crisis has escalated, with Sudan surging to 11.6 million in 2024.
highlights: Sudan's IDP population exploded from 3.5M to 11.6M between 2022 and 2024 following civil war
Syria has maintained ~7M internally displaced since 2013, a persistent crisis
Colombia's long-running displacement peaked near 8.3M in 2020
The total for just these 10 countries exceeds 50 million people displaced within their own borders

visualizations.title: Left Behind
visualizations.description: Out-of-school rate for primary education, earliest vs most recent year, for 15 countries. Slope chart showing dramatic improvements in Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia, with South Sudan as the only country that worsened.
highlights: Guinea reduced its out-of-school rate from 75.8% to 14.7%, one of the largest improvements
India went from 23.4% to just 0.1%, essentially achieving universal primary enrollment
Mozambique dropped from 51.7% to 1.0%, an extraordinary transformation
South Sudan is the only country shown where the rate worsened, from 55.3% to 64.7%

visualizations.title: The University Boom
visualizations.description: Gross enrollment ratio in tertiary education for 10 countries from 1990 to latest year. Values above 100% reflect students outside the typical age range. Turkey surged from 12% to 128%, while Ethiopia remains at 8%.
highlights: Turkey's tertiary enrollment surged from 12% to 128%, the most dramatic expansion in the dataset
South Korea crossed 100% enrollment, meaning more students than the traditional college-age population
China went from 3% to 77%, enrolling tens of millions of new university students
Ethiopia remains below 10%, highlighting the persistent higher-education gap in Sub-Saharan Africa

visualizations.title: Living on Credit
visualizations.description: Household debt as % of GDP for 11 countries from 2000 to 2024. Line chart with a vertical annotation marking the 2008 financial crisis. Traces the housing bubble buildup and aftermath in Ireland, Spain, UK, and USA.
highlights: Switzerland leads at ~130% of GDP, the highest household debt ratio among major economies
Ireland's household debt peaked at ~110% during the Celtic Tiger era, then crashed after 2008
Spain and the UK both show dramatic deleveraging after the 2008 crisis
South Korea's household debt has risen steadily to over 100%, bucking the post-crisis deleveraging trend

visualizations.title: The Global Imbalance
visualizations.description: Current account balance as % of GDP for 9 countries, 2000-2030. Surplus countries (Norway, Singapore, Germany) in blue-green tones above the zero line; deficit countries (USA, UK, Turkey) in coral below. Dashed lines for IMF projections.
highlights: Norway's current account surplus peaked at 29.6% of GDP in 2022, driven by energy exports
The United States has run persistent deficits of 2-6% for over two decades
China's surplus moderated from nearly 10% in 2007 to 2-3%, reducing global imbalances
Germany's large surplus (peaked at ~9% in 2016) has been a source of European economic tension

visualizations.title: The Cost of Catastrophe
visualizations.description: Economic damages from natural disasters per year, 1960-2022, aggregated globally. Bar chart with the 6 costliest years annotated: Hurricane Maria (2017), Indian Ocean Tsunami (2004), Hurricane Mitch (1998).
highlights: 2017 was the costliest year, dominated by Hurricane Maria devastating Dominica and Caribbean islands
The 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami caused massive economic damage across multiple small island nations
Small island states suffer disproportionately -- damages often exceed 100% of their GDP
The trend shows increasing economic damages over time as more assets are exposed to natural hazards

visualizations.title: Open Doors
visualizations.description: Top 20 refugee-hosting countries by number of refugees in 2024. Iran leads with 3.49 million, followed by Turkey (2.94M) and Germany (2.75M). Shows which nations bear the burden of hosting displaced populations.
highlights: Iran hosts the most refugees at 3.49 million, primarily from Afghanistan
Uganda (1.76M) and Chad (1.28M) host more refugees than far wealthier nations
Germany (2.75M) and Poland (1.01M) are the top European hosts, driven by Syria and Ukraine crises
The top 20 host countries collectively shelter over 20 million refugees

visualizations.title: Red Ink
visualizations.description: Government fiscal balance (net lending/borrowing) as % of GDP for 23 countries, 2025 IMF estimates. Diverging bar chart from Kuwait's +26.8% surplus to China's -8.6% deficit. Oil exporters dominate the surplus side.
highlights: Kuwait runs the largest fiscal surplus at 26.8% of GDP, driven entirely by oil revenues
China (-8.6%) and Brazil (-8.4%) run the largest deficits among major economies
The United States deficit of -7.4% places it among the world's biggest borrowers
Norway (+12.7%) channels oil wealth into its sovereign wealth fund, maintaining strong surpluses

visualizations.title: The Persistent Plague
visualizations.description: Tuberculosis incidence per 100,000 population for 10 high-burden countries, 2000-2024. South Africa's HIV-fuelled epidemic peaked above 1,200 before plunging. Ethiopia and Russia halved their rates. The Philippines keeps climbing. Nigeria has not budged.
highlights: South Africa's TB rate peaked at 1,230 per 100k in 2010 driven by HIV co-infection, then fell 68% to 389 by 2024
The Philippines is the only high-burden country where incidence is rising, from 590 to 627
Nigeria's rate has been frozen at exactly 219 per 100,000 for 18 consecutive years of reporting
Ethiopia achieved a 65% reduction from 462 to 163, one of Africa's greatest TB success stories

visualizations.title: The Silent Epidemic
visualizations.description: Suicide mortality rates (per 100,000 population) across 31 countries, ranging from Guyana's 76.9 to Kuwait's 0.7. Color-coded by severity: red for rates above 25, amber for 10-25, green below 10.
highlights: Guyana leads at 76.9 per 100,000, more than 100 times higher than Kuwait's 0.7
Former Soviet states cluster in the high-rate tier: Russia (27.1), Ukraine (27.9), Kazakhstan (40.9), Estonia (39.1)
China (38.5) and Hungary (35.9) have unexpectedly high rates among middle-income and EU nations
Gulf states and Caribbean islands cluster near zero, likely reflecting both cultural factors and underreporting

visualizations.title: Europe's Carbon Retreat
visualizations.description: Greenhouse gas emissions per capita (tonnes CO2 equivalent) for 15 EU countries, 1990-2024. Nordic leaders have halved their footprints while coal-dependent economies in Central Europe are still catching up.
highlights: Luxembourg cut emissions from 33.4 to 11.2 tonnes per capita, a 66% reduction -- the steepest in the EU
Sweden has the lowest emissions at 4.5 tonnes, less than half of Germany's 7.8
Poland's trajectory is the flattest, declining only 27% from 12.5 to 9.1 -- still heavily dependent on coal
Ireland bucked the trend by rising from 13.2 to 18.3 during the Celtic Tiger boom before falling back to 10.0

visualizations.title: The Slow Killers
visualizations.description: Probability of dying from non-communicable diseases (cancer, diabetes, cardiovascular, respiratory) between ages 30-70 for 20 countries, earliest vs most recent year. Most nations are winning this fight, but progress varies enormously.
highlights: South Korea cut NCD mortality from 23.2% to 9.2%, the most dramatic improvement in the dataset
Russia's trajectory reversed: NCD mortality rose from 24.4% to 28.0% before recent improvements
Egypt is one of the few countries where NCD mortality worsened, rising from 29.5% to 30.6%
Germany and the USA both achieved significant reductions, but the USA's 2020 data reflects COVID disruption

visualizations.title: Every Breath You Take
visualizations.description: Mean annual PM2.5 air pollution concentration (ug/m3) for 30 countries in 2019. WHO guideline is 5 ug/m3. Only Canada comes close. Most of South Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa exceeds the guideline by 5-10x.
highlights: Cameroon leads at 54.8 ug/m3, nearly 11 times the WHO guideline of 5 ug/m3
Bangladesh (50.1) and Niger (50.7) round out the top three, all in the darkest severity tier
Even wealthy nations like Germany (11.2), France (10.5), and Japan (10.8) exceed the guideline by 2x
Canada (5.2) is the only country approaching compliance with WHO's annual mean target

visualizations.title: Half the Sky, Half the Seats?
visualizations.description: Women's share of seats in national parliaments, earliest available year (~1997) vs 2024, for 25 countries. Rwanda leads at 61%. Japan and India remain below 16%.
highlights: Rwanda leads the world with 61% women in parliament, having surged from 17% after the 1994 genocide
Japan barely moved from 4.6% to 15.7% over 27 years, one of the slowest improvements among democracies
Mexico jumped from 14% to over 50% after adopting gender parity quotas in 2014
Saudi Arabia went from 0% to 20% after women were first allowed to serve in the Shura Council in 2013

visualizations.title: Out of the Smoke
visualizations.description: Access to clean fuels and technologies for cooking (% of population) from 2000 to 2023 for 12 countries. India's transformation from 23% to 77% stands out. Indonesia leapt from 7% to 91%. Ethiopia barely reached 7%.
highlights: Indonesia achieved the most dramatic transformation: from 6.7% to 90.6% clean fuel access in 23 years
India's Ujjwala Yojana program drove access from 22.7% to 76.7%, lifting hundreds of millions out of solid fuel dependence
China went from 40.3% to 88.7%, driven by urbanization and natural gas infrastructure
Ethiopia remains at just 7.1% -- nearly the entire population still cooks with wood, charcoal, or dung

visualizations.title: Stunted Futures
visualizations.description: Prevalence of stunting (low height for age) in children under 5, earliest survey vs most recent, for 15 countries. Bangladesh halved its rate from 71% to 24%. Guatemala and Afghanistan remain above 44%.
highlights: Bangladesh achieved the largest absolute reduction: from 70.9% to 23.6%, lifting millions of children out of chronic malnutrition
Cambodia dropped from 58.6% to 21.9%, one of Southeast Asia's greatest nutrition success stories
Guatemala's rate barely moved from 62.2% to 46.0% over three decades despite being a middle-income country
India reduced stunting from 62.7% to 35.5%, but with 1.4 billion people, millions of children are still affected

visualizations.title: Still Thirsty
visualizations.description: Access to safely managed drinking water, 2000 vs 2024. Slope chart reveals countries going backward -- some now provide safe water to fewer than one in five people.
highlights: 7 countries lost ground on safe water since 2000
Tunisia dropped from 88% to 68% coverage
Togo fell from 23% to just 9%

visualizations.title: Lifelines Home
visualizations.description: Personal remittances as % of GDP, 2000-2024. For Honduras, El Salvador, and Guatemala, diaspora earnings are not a supplement -- they are the economy.
highlights: Honduras remittances surged from 7% to 26% of GDP
COVID paradox: remittances rose during 2020 lockdowns
Central American economies deeply dependent on diaspora

visualizations.title: The Global Fattening
visualizations.description: Obesity prevalence (BMI >= 30) among adults, 1990-2022. Pacific islands lead at 50-70%, while East Asia remains low but rising. Almost every country is getting heavier.
highlights: Nauru obesity rate exceeds 60%
Gulf states cluster at 30-48%
Japan and South Korea remain under 7% but rising

visualizations.title: Roads That Kill
visualizations.description: Estimated road traffic death rate per 100,000 population for 39 countries (2021). A 25-fold gap separates Guinea (37.4) from Norway (1.5). Color-coded by severity: red (>20), orange (10-20), yellow (5-10), green (<5).
highlights: Guinea has the world's highest road death rate at 37.4 per 100,000, nearly 25 times Norway's 1.5
The United States (14.2) has a death rate 7 times higher than peer nations like Sweden (2.1) and Norway (1.5)
Sub-Saharan Africa dominates the critical tier: Guinea, Libya, Haiti, Zimbabwe all above 25 per 100k
Singapore (1.9) and Norway (1.5) demonstrate that near-zero road deaths are achievable with investment in infrastructure and enforcement

visualizations.title: Up in Smoke
visualizations.description: Tobacco smoking prevalence across 12 countries, 2000-2030. Most nations are stubbing out the habit, but Egypt lights up more than ever.
highlights: Japan smoking halved from 52% to under 8%
Egypt projected to reach 53% by 2030
Indonesia barely budged at 30%

visualizations.title: Safer Beginnings
visualizations.description: Births attended by skilled health staff -- slope chart comparing earliest vs latest survey. Enormous gains in countries like Cambodia (+55pp) and Egypt (+63pp).
highlights: Burundi gained 68 percentage points in skilled birth attendance
Ethiopia rose from 6.5% to nearly 50%
Ecuador and Colombia near universal coverage

visualizations.title: Cities of Informality
visualizations.description: Slum population as share of urban residents, 2000 vs 2022. Connected dot plot reveals two decades of progress or backsliding across 17 countries.
highlights: Egypt slashed slum population from 42% to under 4%
Cambodia improved from 85% to 42%
Iraq worsened from 34% to 49%

visualizations.title: Too Young, Too Soon
visualizations.description: Adolescent fertility rate (births per 1,000 women ages 15-19) from 1960 to 2023. India and Bhutan achieved 90%+ reductions; Central African Republic barely changed.
highlights: Bhutan cut adolescent fertility by 92% since 1960
Central African Republic still at 163 births per 1000
China and South Korea below 6 births per 1000

visualizations.title: Learning to Read the World
visualizations.description: Adult literacy rate changes across 18 countries. Connected dot plot showing earliest vs latest survey values -- from Chad's 31% to China's 97%.
highlights: Burkina Faso literacy surged from 9% to 41%
Congo DR and Angola saw slight declines
China and Indonesia now above 96%

visualizations.title: The Long Climb
visualizations.description: Life expectancy at birth across regions and countries from WHO data. A story of convergence as developing nations close the gap with the rich world.
highlights: Sub-Saharan Africa recovered from HIV/AIDS crisis
East Asia converging with Europe
Global life expectancy gap narrowing

visualizations.title: The Scars of Crisis: Europe's Unemployment Trajectories
visualizations.description: EU unemployment rates for major economies showing the 2008 financial crisis, eurozone crisis, and COVID shock. Greece and Spain peaked above 25%.
highlights: Greece unemployment peaked above 27% in 2013
Germany steadily fell from 11% to under 4%
COVID caused synchronized spike in 2020

visualizations.title: The Renewable Divergence
visualizations.description: Renewable energy share of total final energy consumption across countries. Some nations lead the clean transition while others remain fossil-dependent.
highlights: Iceland and Norway lead with 70%+ renewable share
Oil states near zero renewables
Global average slowly rising

visualizations.title: When the World Economy Stumbles
visualizations.description: GDP growth rates for major economies showing synchronized recessions in 2008-2009 and 2020. India and China's growth trajectories diverge from the West.
highlights: 2020 COVID crash was deepest synchronized recession
China never went negative during 2008 crisis
India's V-shaped COVID recovery

visualizations.title: Where People Move
visualizations.description: Net migration patterns showing which countries gain and lose people. Diverging bar chart of 2022 flows plus trend lines for key countries.
highlights: Germany gained 981K people in 2022 from Ukrainian crisis
India is largest emigration source at -1.35M
Japan shifting to net immigration

visualizations.title: Under Pressure
visualizations.description: Raised blood pressure prevalence across countries and regions. The silent epidemic affecting over a billion people worldwide.

South Korea and Canada lowest among surveyed
Global average declining but slowly

visualizations.title: Guarding the Blue
visualizations.description: Marine key biodiversity areas covered by protected areas, tracking ocean conservation progress under SDG 14.

Global marine protection expanding rapidly

visualizations.title: The Openness Divide
visualizations.description: Trade as a share of GDP over time, contrasting structurally open economies with larger countries that remain relatively closed.
highlights: Hong Kong and Ireland trade several times their GDP
China surged after opening, then partially retrenched
Large economies like Japan and Brazil stay comparatively closed

visualizations.title: When Health Costs Crush Households
visualizations.description: Population spending large shares of earnings on out-of-pocket health expenses. A measure of financial protection failure in health systems.
highlights: Some countries see 20%+ of population crushed by health costs
Universal health coverage reduces catastrophic spending
Low-income countries most affected

visualizations.title: Capital Flows Across Borders
visualizations.description: Foreign direct investment inflows as a share of GDP, showing globalization waves, crises, and sharp reversals.
highlights: Ireland is an extreme FDI outlier
China and India show distinct opening trajectories
Emerging markets experience repeated boom-bust cycles

visualizations.title: What Nations Invest in Education
visualizations.description: Government expenditure on education as % of GDP. Bar chart comparing 20 countries from spenders like Norway to underinvestors.
highlights: Nordic countries spend 6-8% of GDP on education
Several developing nations spend under 3%
No clear correlation between spending and outcomes

visualizations.title: The Broadband Divide
visualizations.description: Fixed broadband subscriptions per 100 inhabitants showing the digital divide across development levels.
highlights: Rich nations at 40+ subscriptions per 100
Sub-Saharan Africa still under 1 per 100
South Korea leads globally

visualizations.title: The Price of Catastrophe
visualizations.description: The costliest country-year disaster losses since 2005, alongside rising global annual totals.
highlights: Colombia and the United States dominate the top-loss years
A handful of disaster events account for outsized damage
Global losses rise sharply after 2014

visualizations.title: Women in the Boardroom
visualizations.description: Proportion of women in managerial positions across countries. Lollipop chart showing progress toward gender parity in leadership.
highlights: No country has reached full parity in management
Eastern European nations often outperform Western ones
Progress has been gradual across most regions

visualizations.title: The Weight of Forests
visualizations.description: Above-ground biomass density in forests, comparing 2000 and 2020 across selected countries and continents.
highlights: European forests generally grew denser over two decades
South Korea is a standout reforestation story
Some tropical countries saw losses or stagnation

visualizations.title: Europe's Poverty Divide
visualizations.description: Population at risk of poverty or social exclusion in Europe, comparing 2015 with 2023.
highlights: Romania and Bulgaria posted large improvements
A few countries moved backward despite high income levels
European poverty-risk trajectories remain highly uneven

visualizations.title: The Air We Breathe Kills
visualizations.description: Deaths attributable to air pollution by country, highlighting the concentration of the global burden.
highlights: India is a clear outlier in total deaths
The burden is concentrated in large middle-income countries
Air pollution remains a major health risk across very different regions

visualizations.title: The Long Retreat of Child Malnutrition
visualizations.description: Earliest-versus-latest underweight prevalence among children under 5, showing where malnutrition has fallen fastest.
highlights: Bangladesh and China made dramatic long-run progress
Some countries show only modest improvement
A few cases stalled or worsened despite global gains

visualizations.title: The Relative Poverty Gap
visualizations.description: Population living below half the median income across countries, using the latest recent data points.
highlights: Latin American countries dominate the highest rates
The United States stands out among rich countries
Nordic and Central Asian countries cluster at the low end

visualizations.title: Living Longer, Living Healthier
visualizations.description: Healthy life expectancy at birth, comparing earliest and latest available values across countries.
highlights: Sub-Saharan African countries posted some of the biggest gains
High-income Asian countries remain at the top
The United States saw only limited improvement

visualizations.title: The Classroom Boom
visualizations.description: Gross secondary school enrollment over five decades, showing dramatic educational expansion in developing countries.
highlights: Developing countries closed much of the enrollment gap
High-income systems have long hovered near or above full enrollment
The global education expansion is visible over half a century

visualizations.title: Can They Read? Can They Count?
visualizations.description: Latest available proficiency in reading and mathematics, split by whether countries clear the 50% threshold.
highlights: In many countries, fewer than half of students meet minimum proficiency
East Asian systems dominate the top of the ranking
The 50% threshold separates majority-success from majority-failure systems

visualizations.title: The Legal Landscape of Gender Equality
visualizations.description: Country scores for legal frameworks supporting gender equality in labour-force participation and public life.
highlights: Some countries now achieve a perfect score of 100
Many countries remain stuck in partial middle-band compliance
A tail of countries still has very weak legal protection

visualizations.title: Carbon Worlds Apart
visualizations.description: CO2 emissions per capita across countries. Qatar emits 30+ tons per person while most African nations emit under 1.
highlights: Gulf states emit 30+ tons CO2 per capita
China surpassed EU average in mid-2000s
Sub-Saharan Africa barely registers on carbon charts

visualizations.title: The Diabetes Divide
visualizations.description: Age-standardized diabetes prevalence across 12 countries from 1990-2022. Gulf states and Pacific islands face an epidemic while Asia remains lower.
highlights: Kuwait diabetes prevalence exceeds 20%
Marshall Islands among highest in the world
China and India rising from low base

visualizations.title: A World Apart in Every Glass
visualizations.description: Total alcohol per capita consumption across 22 countries. Romania drinks 1700x more than Afghanistan per person.
highlights: Romania leads at 17 litres per capita
Muslim-majority nations near zero
Eastern Europe dominates global drinking

visualizations.title: The Doctor Divide
visualizations.description: Medical doctors per 10,000 population. Cuba has 85 doctors per 10,000 while Niger has 0.4 -- a 218x gap.
highlights: Cuba leads with 85 doctors per 10,000
Niger has only 0.4 doctors per 10,000
Many African nations below WHO minimum threshold

visualizations.title: The Measles Shield
visualizations.description: Measles MCV1 immunization coverage trends. Some countries approaching 95% herd immunity threshold while others fall behind.
highlights: 95% threshold needed for herd immunity
Several countries saw COVID-era vaccination drops
Sub-Saharan Africa making steady progress

visualizations.title: Lighting Up the Countryside

highlights: Bangladesh rural electrification: 3% to 99.6%
Burkina Faso essentially zero progress in 30 years

visualizations.title: Hospital Beds Around the World
visualizations.description: Hospital beds per 1,000 people. Post-Soviet states declining from communist-era highs while most of Africa lacks basic capacity.
highlights: Japan has 13 beds per 1,000 people
Post-Soviet states declining from high base
Most African nations have under 1 bed per 1,000

visualizations.title: The Innovation Divide
visualizations.description: R&D expenditure as % of GDP. Israel and South Korea lead at 5%+; China's rapid climb; most of Africa invests under 0.5%.
highlights: Israel spends over 5% of GDP on R&D
China's R&D spending surpassed EU average

visualizations.description: Prevalence of undernourishment trends. Some making progress (China, Brazil), others worsening (Yemen, Venezuela).
highlights: China virtually eliminated undernourishment
Yemen crisis pushed hunger above 30%
Global hunger declining but unevenly

visualizations.title: Financial Deepening
visualizations.description: Domestic credit to private sector as % of GDP. Hong Kong at 231% vs Ghana at 9% -- a 25x gap in financial depth.
highlights: China's credit boom doubled after 2008
Hong Kong leads at over 200% of GDP

visualizations.title: The Tax Divide

highlights: Nordic countries collect 25-32% of GDP in taxes
Low-income countries often below 10%
Tax capacity a key development indicator

visualizations.title: The Clean Energy Revolution

highlights: Denmark leads with 60%+ non-hydro renewables
China's renewable electricity growing rapidly
Most developing nations still near zero

highlights: Scandinavia leads with 100+ nurses per 10,000

Nurse-to-doctor ratios vary enormously by country

visualizations.description: IMF real GDP growth with projections through 2030. Solid lines for observed data, dashed for forecasts.
highlights: China and India lead growth projections

visualizations.title: Europe's Innovation Divide

highlights: Sweden overtook Germany as top EU patenter per capita

Persistent North-South innovation gap

highlights: Bulgaria lost 4+ years of life expectancy gains to COVID
Spain dropped 1.6 years in 2020 alone

highlights: Global asylum seekers grew 9x from 950K to 8.3M
Venezuela alone accounts for 1.36M by 2024
2015 European migration crisis visible as sharp spike

highlights: China's private debt surged from 100% to 230% of GDP

highlights: Germany became top EU destination after 2015
Spain and Italy major Mediterranean gateways
Immigration patterns shifted with each crisis

visualizations.title: The Rhythm of European Growth
visualizations.description: Real GDP per capita growth for 8 EU economies, 2000-2025. Greece's prolonged contraction, Poland's resilience in 2009, Spain's deep COVID hit.
highlights: Poland uniquely avoided recession in 2009
Greece contracted for 7 straight years
Spain dropped -11.4% in 2020

visualizations.title: Left Behind

highlights: Malta: 54% to 9.5% early school leavers
21 of 34 countries meet EU 2030 target of 9%
Turkey and Romania still above 15%

visualizations.title: The Long Road to Clean Water
visualizations.description: Access to basic drinking water services across countries. Progress from the MDG era to the SDG era.
highlights: Several countries achieved near-universal water access
Sub-Saharan Africa still lags significantly
Progress accelerated after 2000

visualizations.title: Years Left After 60

highlights: Singapore leads at 27.9 years after 60

highlights: Comoros primary completion up 41 percentage points
DR Congo and Ethiopia gained 36+ points each

visualizations.title: Under the Surface
visualizations.description: Nitrate in groundwater across EU countries. Spain has the highest levels; Latvia and Sweden the cleanest. Bulgaria's rising trend is concerning.
highlights: Spain highest nitrate at 31.7 mg/L
Bulgaria rising trend most concerning in EU
Latvia and Sweden cleanest groundwater

visualizations.title: Corporate Leverage
visualizations.description: Nonfinancial corporate debt as % of GDP for 10 major economies. France leads at 155%, China's steep rise to 144%, Japan deleveraging from bubble peak.
highlights: France corporate debt reached 155% of GDP
China's corporate debt surged from near zero to 144%
Japan deleveraged after 1990s bubble

visualizations.title: Divergent Destinies: European Population Since 1990
visualizations.description: EU population trends showing divergent paths: Germany growing via migration, Bulgaria shrinking by a third, Poland's post-accession dip.
highlights: Bulgaria lost a third of its population since 1990
Germany grew through immigration despite low birth rates
Eastern Europe steadily shrinking

visualizations.title: The Price of a Longer Life
visualizations.description: Health expenditure per capita vs. life expectancy at birth across 85 countries in 2021. The logarithmic scatter reveals that the first few hundred dollars of health spending per person produce the steepest gains in longevity, while wealthy nations spending thousands more see only marginal improvements.
visualizations.generatedCode:
The Price of a Longer Life

Health expenditure per capita vs. life expectancy at birth, 85 countries, 2021. Logarithmic x-axis reveals that initial health spending buys dramatic gains, but returns flatten sharply beyond ~$1,000 per person.

Source: World Bank -- Health Expenditure per Capita (SH.XPD.CHEX.PC.CD) and Life Expectancy at Birth (SP.DYN.LE00.IN)
highlights: Japan leads with 84.5 years of life expectancy on $4,844 per capita health spending -- outperforming countries that spend 50% more
Bangladesh achieves 71 years of life expectancy on just $56 per capita, the most efficient health outcome among South Asian nations
Central African Republic is the starkest outlier at 40.3 years and $46 per capita, nearly 20 years below its spending peers
Botswana and Eswatini spend $300-500 per capita but achieve lower life expectancy than countries spending $30, likely reflecting the HIV/AIDS burden

visualizations.title: Saving Children: Under-5 Mortality, 2000 vs 2020
visualizations.description: Slope chart comparing under-5 mortality rates across 18 countries between 2000 and 2020, showing dramatic declines in child deaths across the developing world alongside rare reversals in small island nations.
visualizations.generatedCode:
Saving Children: Under-5 Mortality, 2000 vs 2020

Under-5 deaths per 1,000 live births -- 18 countries showing dramatic declines and rare reversals

Source: World Bank -- Mortality rate, under-5 (per 1,000 live births) -- indicator SH.DYN.MORT
highlights: Angola saw the largest absolute decline, dropping from 203 to 72 deaths per 1,000 live births (-131 deaths in 20 years)
Cambodia achieved a 76% reduction, from 106 to just 26 deaths per 1,000 -- one of the steepest percentage drops globally
China reached near-developed-nation levels at 7.5 per 1,000, down 80% from 36.7 in 2000
Dominica and Fiji are rare reversals where under-5 mortality actually increased between 2000 and 2020

visualizations.title: The Carbon Shift: Who Drives Global CO2 Emissions?
visualizations.description: CO2 emissions from fuel combustion (millions of tonnes) for the eight largest emitters, 2000 to 2022. China overtook the United States around 2006 and now emits more than twice as much, while India has tripled its output.
visualizations.generatedCode:
The Carbon Shift: Who Drives Global CO2 Emissions?

CO2 emissions from fuel combustion (millions of tonnes) for the eight largest emitters, 2000 to 2022. China overtook the United States around 2006 and now emits more than twice as much, while India has tripled its output.

Source: UN SDG 9.4.1 -- CO2 Emissions from Fuel Combustion (EN_ATM_CO2)
highlights: China's emissions tripled from 3,200 to 10,600 MT between 2000 and 2022, becoming the world's largest emitter by overtaking the US around 2006
The United States decreased emissions by 20%, from 5,700 to 4,550 MT, the largest absolute decline among the top eight
India nearly tripled from 900 to 2,500 MT, emerging as the third largest emitter and the fastest-growing major source
Germany and Japan both reduced emissions by 15-25%, while Russia and Canada remained relatively flat over the period

visualizations.title: The Mobile Revolution: From Zero to Ubiquity
visualizations.description: Mobile cellular subscriptions per 100 people for 9 countries from 1990 to 2023, showing how the mobile phone went from a Finnish novelty to a globally ubiquitous technology in just three decades.
highlights: Finland was the early leader with 5 subscriptions per 100 people in 1990 when most countries had zero
Japan now leads at 178 per 100, meaning the average person holds nearly two subscriptions
Kenya went from near-zero in 2000 to 121 per 100 in 2023, leapfrogging landline infrastructure entirely
India plateaued around 80 per 100 -- lower penetration in a country of 1.4 billion still means over a billion subscriptions
Ethiopia remains the furthest behind at 57 per 100, but is the fastest-growing line on the chart

visualizations.title: Youth Unemployment Rates Around the World, 2024
visualizations.description: Horizontal bar chart comparing youth unemployment (ages 15-24) across 28 countries, from Cambodia at 0.7% to Djibouti at 76.5%
visualizations.generatedCode:
Youth Unemployment Rates Around the World, 2024

Unemployment as % of labor force ages 15-24 (ILO modeled estimates). 28 countries spanning the full range.

Source: World Bank / International Labour Organization (ILO)
highlights: Djibouti has the highest youth unemployment at 76.5%, over 100x higher than Cambodia at 0.7%
Sub-Saharan Africa dominates both extremes -- Guinea-Bissau at 2.8% and Congo Rep. at 40.6%
France at 18.6% has higher youth unemployment than India at 15.7%
Cambodia, Guinea-Bissau, and Cote d Ivoire all keep youth unemployment below 5%

visualizations.title: Sanitation Access: Two Decades of Progress (and Stagnation)
visualizations.description: Slope chart comparing basic sanitation access in 2000 vs 2022 for 18 countries. Lines connect each country across 22 years, color-coded by improvement level: dramatic gains (Cambodia, India, Indonesia), large gains (Bangladesh, China, Bolivia, Afghanistan), moderate gains (Ghana, Colombia, Brazil, Kenya), stagnation (Haiti, Ethiopia, Chad), high baselines (Germany, Japan, Egypt), and decline (DR Congo).
visualizations.generatedCode:
Sanitation Access: Two Decades of Progress (and Stagnation)

People using at least basic sanitation services (% of population), 2000 vs 2022. Lines connect each country across 22 years.

Source: World Bank / WHO-UNICEF Joint Monitoring Programme (JMP) | Indicator: SH.STA.BASS.ZS
highlights: Cambodia vaulted from 10% to 77% sanitation coverage -- the largest gain of any country shown, at nearly 67 percentage points
India achieved a 63-point leap (15% to 78%), reflecting massive government sanitation campaigns like Swachh Bharat
DR Congo is the only country that went backward, falling from 24% to 16% amid decades of conflict
Ethiopia and Chad remain below 13% despite two decades passing, showing the deepest sanitation poverty
Japan and Germany were already near-universal in 2000 and stayed there, illustrating the ceiling effect

visualizations.title: The U-Shape of Female Labor: Work, Wealth, and Gender
visualizations.description: Scatter plot of female labor force participation vs GDP per capita (log scale) for 85 countries in 2023. Reveals the well-documented U-shaped pattern: women in low-income agrarian economies participate at high rates, participation drops sharply in middle-income countries (especially in the Middle East and South Asia), then rises again in wealthy nations.
visualizations.generatedCode:
The U-Shape of Female Labor: Work, Wealth, and Gender

Female labor force participation (%) vs GDP per capita (log scale), 2023. Women in the poorest and richest countries work at higher rates; participation dips sharply in middle-income nations, especially in the Middle East and South Asia.

Source: World Bank SL.TLF.CACT.FE.ZS and NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, 2023
highlights: Afghanistan has the lowest female LFP at 5.2%, while Burundi leads at 80% -- a 15x gap driven by cultural and institutional factors, not income alone
Middle East and North Africa cluster at the bottom: Iraq (10.9%), Iran (14.1%), Algeria (14.3%), and Jordan (15.9%) all fall below 16%
Sub-Saharan African countries dominate the upper-left quadrant, with participation rates of 55-80% despite GDP per capita under $3,000
The U-shape is visible: high LFP in poor agrarian economies, a dip in middle-income countries, and recovery in wealthy nations like Iceland (70.4%) and Canada (61.1%)

visualizations.title: The Green Revolution: Who Benefited, Who Was Left Behind
visualizations.description: Line chart tracking cereal yield (kg per hectare) from 1961 to 2023 across 8 countries, showing how high-yield crop varieties transformed agriculture in Asia and Latin America while parts of Sub-Saharan Africa lagged behind.
highlights: China increased cereal yields over 5x from 1,193 to 6,418 kg/ha, the most dramatic transformation among major countries
Indonesia nearly quadrupled yields from 1,542 to 5,829 kg/ha, driven by high-yield rice varieties from IRRI
Kenya's 2023 yield (1,758 kg/ha) is barely above India's 1961 starting point (947 kg/ha), illustrating the divergence
Ethiopia shows a recent uptick from roughly 1,100 in the early 2000s to 2,864 by 2023, suggesting the Green Revolution may finally be reaching East Africa

visualizations.title: The Shrinking Share: Freshwater Per Capita, 1961 -- 2022
visualizations.description: Renewable internal freshwater resources per person (cubic meters) across 9 countries from 1961 to 2022. Uses a log scale to show how population growth has driven per-capita freshwater down everywhere, from water-abundant Iceland to water-scarce Bahrain.
highlights: Jordan lost 92% of its per-capita freshwater since 1961, falling from 760 to just 61 cubic meters -- deep into absolute water scarcity
Bahrain has the lowest freshwater per person of any country shown at 2.6 cubic meters, down from 23 in 1961, an 89% decline
Iceland still has 445,000 cubic meters per person despite a 53% decline, illustrating the vast inequality in global water endowments
India crossed below the 1,000 cubic meter water-stress threshold around 2013 and continues to decline toward scarcity
Kenya fell from 2,592 to 382 cubic meters per person, an 85% decline driven by one of Africa's fastest population growth rates

visualizations.title: The Many Speeds of Globalization
visualizations.description: Trade (exports + imports) as a share of GDP for 10 countries from 1960 to 2024, revealing how trading hubs, liberalizers, and protectionist economies followed vastly different paths into the global economy.
highlights: Hong Kong trade reached 360% of GDP by 2024, reflecting its role as a re-export hub where goods pass through multiple times
Ireland surged from 80% to 246% after the 1990s as multinationals routed intellectual property exports through Dublin
China's trade share rose from 5% in 1960 to a peak of 65% around 2006, then retreated to 37% as its domestic economy matured
Argentina and Brazil remain among the most closed large economies, with trade shares of 28% and 36% respectively in 2024
South Korea's transformation from 36% in 1970 to 85% today tracks its rise from agricultural economy to semiconductor powerhouse
